title: Peer TV South Tyrol — live segment
text: sea level. Today we do not take advantage of the opportunity to stop at the restaurant and hotel of the same name but sit at one of the tables with benches along the banks. And now it's picnic time. And after a souvenir photo we set off on our return journey, this time along path number 10 which takes us directly back to la villa. We continue straight ahead on an asphalt road which then changes to a forest road. Here we are accompanied by views of the surrounding mountain massives and then shady sections of the forest. The trail is also popular in cyclists and is suitable for strollers. When we reach the village we can already see the church, our starting point in the distance. The height difference of this circular route is 250 meters with a walking time of one and a half hours making it suitable for both a day trip and a simple walk. A lively charming wine village with its gewurztraminer and extensive vineyards. Join us as we discover the pleasant village square and lots of culture and tradition. Welcome to Termino in the south Tyrolian lowlands. Termino resides 276 meters above sea level in a mild climate south of Lake Caldero and surrounded by vineyards. The gewurtztraminer grows here on the slopes and is arguably one of the world's most famous white wines. As the name suggests, the wine is said to have originated here. However, the village with its 3,300 inhabitants is full of surprises. The best way to uncover these surprises is to meet with a traminer. So we arranged to meet Lenz Kopelstädter, a best-selling author and journalist at the Hormat Museum in the center of the village. Meeting at the entrance to the museum is the reason for meeting here because it's been ages since I'd visited the museum. The museum is located in a historic building that served as a bathhouse during the 15th and 16th centuries. In the museum, one can see traditions such as the famous Egetman Carnival procession. On display are also old crafts, methods of agriculture, and of course the traminer wine, which originated in the municipality. Hermann Toll is the museum curator and explains this in greater detail to us. We are looking at an old bottle of traminer from the Elzenbaum, bottled in 1886. It's labeled as a traminer, but it's probably a gewurtztraminer. The difference between the two is that the gewurtztraminer originated during the Ice Age in Germany as a mutation. As the ice receded, the gewurtztraminer returned to Austria and South Tyrol. This wine is produced worldwide in the United States, New Zealand, South Africa, Germany, and other countries. Before we set off for Lenz Kopelstetter's next favorite place, we take a look around the village square and take a leisurely coffee break. Our next destination is the church of St. James on Castellatz Hill. This is one of my favorite spots overlooking the houses of Termenio. Here's the church of Castellatz, which contains beautiful frescoes. The views from here are amazing and provide lots of inspiration. Walking along the vineyards, one is always rewarded with stunning panoramas. The church of St. James on Castellatz Hill was first mentioned in historical records, dating back to 1214. But the chapel was probably built in the 11th century and around 1220, the Romanesque fresco cycle was added. At the end of our little trip to Termenio, it's time to taste the famous gewurtztraminer we have seen and heard about in the Termenio winery. This architectural masterpiece is the entrance to Termenio. It's a beautiful place where locals and tourists meet. The farmers also come here and drink a glass of wine. It's also a place from my childhood. My grandfather brought his wine here. Termenio in the South Tyrolian lowlands is a municipality that has much to offer. Southern landscapes combined with culture, tradition and enjoyment. Astro Village sounds almost romantic. In the towns of San Valentino in Campo and Colepietra, everything revolves around the cosmos, even on excursions. At the planetarium of San Valentino in Campo, we make our way to the first station of the path of the stars, which combines seven stations related to the universe. Station number four, the astronomical observatory can already be seen from the bus stop. We're at the beginning of the path of stars, which leads from San Valentino to Colepietra. This structure is meant to symbolize a giant camera. How was the universe discovered hundreds of years ago? How did man try to explain the starry sky? Through telescopes, spotting scopes and, indeed, cameras. That's why the idea of being able to zoom into space through a giant camera is so cool. And now we're going to do it. Come along. The four holes in the ceiling depict a little deeper constellation. The North Star, the last star on the rudder, points our way through the gigantic camera. Along the hiking trail, we reached the next station, located on an open-air platform, the date sundial. The date sundial works differently from a classical sundial. It's more concerned with the solar cycle through the seasons or months. The date table is oriented exactly in the direction of the North Pole, so the table also looks towards the North Star. The ring around it is placed at the same level as the celestial equator. And because the Sun is in a different position during the seasons or months, the shadow cast by the ring also changes. There is also a revolving depture, which allows stargazing during the night. It is also a perfect place to rest during the day. Station number three is a star map, which helps to identify the constellations. There are, in fact, 88 constellations that only very few people know these days. Through them, one can recognize the cardinal points and determine the season. The observatory on San Valentino also features a solar observatory, with which it is possible to observe the surface of our sun. Regardless of the path of the stars, it is an interesting leisure activity for all universe enthusiasts. Navigators used to find their way thanks to the starry sky. In this context, most of us are quite familiar with the North Star, which helps determine North pretty accurately. When we search for the big dipper in the night sky, in the box of which there are these two outer stars, Merak and Dube, if we count this distance five times in a straight line, we find the North Star. At night, one can cast a glance through the telescope, pointed in the direction of the North Star. By day, however, the magnificent view of Calepietra is enough to make a trip here worthwhile. The space rocket in the trees near Calepietra is a multi-storey treehouse, in which to go wild. From the information board, you can learn curious facts about space rockets, a marvel of technology. We are now at the last station, by the mini path of the stars, and this is a very interesting story. We start with our star, the sun, and go into the universe. Here, over a short distance, hence the name mini path of the stars, eleven chosen stars are spaced out from each other in the correct proportion. In the mini path of the stars, one can understand the immense distance in the universe. The stars are in scale with each other, and even our sun can be observed next to each star in proportion. However, this station also hides another peculiarity, visible only at night. The spheres light up in the correct luminous radiance, because, depending on the heat of the surface, the color of each star is different. Opportunity to get to know both, towns and their surroundings, and to reflect on the universe's mysteries. No guided tours are offered, but it is not necessary. as the signs of simple and clear, perfect for families. So, the next time we raise our eyes to the sky, we will certainly see more than just sparkling dots of light. Is it ancestral places of worship, a unique natural setting, or simply a beautiful place that makes us feel good? Places with a strong sense of spirituality have always impressed and inspired people. One of these is St. Hippolytus in Narano in the municipality of Tezimo. Let's immerse ourselves in its mystical atmosphere. The hill of St. Hippolytus in Narano, a hamlet of Tezimo, marks the highest point of the Tezimo Plateau. With its imposing height and breathtaking 360-degree view, it is not only a popular hiking destination, but has always aroused spiritual feelings too. The first traces of settlement date back to the Neolithic period and then to the 4th century BC. In addition to knives, arrowheads and clothing clasps, numerous remains of dwellings and also a place of worship, dating from between the 4th and 2nd centuries BC have been found. Today, the small church of St. Hippolytus, which is mentioned in 1288, but certainly much older, marks the top of this particular hill. The little church shows how this place has maintained a spiritual influence over people ever since Christian times. Here we can contemplate not only the silence, but also the breathtaking panorama, which stretches from the Tezimo group above Merano to Bolzano, and Tezimo halfway up the slope. This is why St. Hippolytus is also known as the Royal Lodge of the Borg Graviato. Listen to the silence and to yourself. Tuning into the strong spiritual atmosphere of this place is really worth the effort. Above Merano, in a beautiful sunny position and with the best view, lies Sheina. The village extends from the vineyard on the slope to the summit cross, high in the mountains. Sheina offers an extensive hiking area at all altitudes for casual hikers, families, and advanced mountain hikers. Sheina invites you to explore short and long hikes from spring until late autumn. We set off today from the Mit de Platvec path. The sunny panoramic promenade leads from the village centre down to the spa town of Merano. The trail leads through apple and vineyards and offers a beautiful 360-degree panoramic view. The Meadowpath is another good way to get to know the village and the surrounding area. The trail starts in the centre of the village and leads around the town past all the cultural sites. Among them are the Mausoleum, Castle Sheina, the parish churches, and St. George's Church. The Meadowpath is stunning in spring when the apple trees bloom. The Sheina-Valvec Trail offers two great viewing platforms above the Katzenleiter and St. George. Another notable highlight of this trail is the waterfall. We are now off on a cultural hike. The well-loved Farm Trail is an easily walkable, themed trail connecting several farms on Mount Sheina. Information panels along the way offer valuable insights into South Tyrol's rural life. Not far off are always magnificent views of the Merano Valley Basin. The Taza High Route leads us a bit further up. It starts at the Taza Alm Alpine pasture, a paradise for families, and takes hikers in easy ups and downs through forests and clearings up to the Ghistaya Mountain Inn. During the two-and-a-half-hour walk we can always admire Merano from a bird's-eye view. Next, we're in the middle of the Punta Cervina hiking area. This can be reached with the Hirza Cable Car, the Verdines Talla Cable Car, and the Talgruba chairlift. Up here, we go on a so-called mountain hut hopping tour. From the Hirza Cable Car, we continue via the Hirza Mountain Hut and Malga Rezegar to the Malga Hinteregga, the Malga Madalm, and finally via the Malga Gompen back to the middle station. This hike is lovely during the Alpine Rose Blossom in June. And last but not least, we undertake a mountain tour up to the Hönig Spitzer Peak, at 2,698 meters, and the Hirza Spitzer Peak at 2,781 meters. During this hike, a fantastic valley basin, Gruppo di Tessa, the Venosta Valley, and the Dolomites. For this tour, sure-footedness and a head for heights are absolutely necessary. From the village center to the summit cross, hiking in Shena is possible at all altitudes. We're hiking through one of the most fascinating natural landscapes in South Tyrol, the Altavia Meranese. Our route takes us through the Val di Tel, an unspoilt valley with meadows and rushing streams. Here surrounded by nature, you immediately get the sense that nature itself is in charge. Parchines is famous for its water. The Parchines waterfall plunges an incredible 97 meters. It's no surprise that it's considered one of the most beautiful in the entire Alpine region. The destination today is Malganasaite, a classic stopover on one of the most popular sections of the Merano Trail. Meters nestled in nature with mountain views, surrounded by fragrant woods and friendly curious goats. Here I'm greeted not only with warmth, but with delicious food too. So, did you enjoy it? It was absolutely delicious. Thank you, Paul. Good, I'm glad. What cakes do you have today? Today we have apple strudel, buckwheat cake or cheesecake. But if you like, I could also make you a Kaiser Schmarne. That would be fantastic. I'd love to make it with you. May I? Of course you can. Come with me. Let's go to the kitchen and make a Kaiser Schmarne. Okay. Paul, today we're making a Kaiser Schmarne. What ingredients have you got ready? For the Kaiser Schmarne, we need just a little milk, flour, salt and a few eggs. You'll have to go and get the eggs. All right, I'm on my way. A real treat. Fresh eggs from hens raised in a beautiful environment, surrounded by nature. Here we are, they're just laid them. Yes, more than we need, I'd say. How many portions are we making today? One for you and one for me. Perfect. You've made me want some too. Now for the batter. Do you have exact quantities? No, I just eyeball it. Okay. For two people, the ideal recipe calls for three to four eggs, 200 millilitres of milk and 100 grams of flour. Paul starts by mixing the milk and flour until he has a smooth, even batter. There, see? The consistency is perfect now. Now we add a little salt.
